bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., 'Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114 (Dialekt von Nottinghamshire?),' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 8 (1885): 102-196.
bibliographic.title: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114 (Dialekt von Nottinghamshire?)

entities.label: Carl Horstmann's 1885 Prosalegenden from MS. Douce 114
notes: This is the immediate printed source represented by CME00060.

The German introduction occupies pp. 102-106; Elizabeth pp. 107-118; Christina pp. 119-134; Marie pp. 134-184; the Catherine letter pp. 184-195; and the closing compiler apology pp. 195-196. Pages 134, 184, and 195 are shared boundaries.
Horstmann maps the four medieval items to manuscript fols. 1-89v and prints the adjacent Seven Points separately in Anglia 10 (1888).

rights.basis: University of Michigan asserts No Copyright-United States for the CME collection; Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the US for the scanned volume.
rights.component: 1885 article text, editorial readings, introduction, and notes

access.accessMethod: Consult the exact catalog and arrange Special Collections access under current reader and conservation rules

access.format: Reference PDF scans or high-resolution colour TIFF images
notes: Request the complete physical codex, not only the target leaves: upper and lower covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and later endleaves, blanks, every recto and verso of all 152 currently cataloged leaves, inserts or foldouts, and edges when offered.

Identify the work portion separately as edition-reported fols. 1r-89v, with fol. 89v shared by the opening of Seven Points; ask staff to reconcile the Biblissima 87r endpoint and the 150-versus-152 leaf count.

Current listed prices are £1 per reference scan for non-Oxford users; high-resolution colour starts at £27 for the first image and £7.50 per subsequent image from the same item, with large orders negotiated. Conservation or legal limits may apply.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century English parchment codex preserving the unique known Middle English copies of the four texts edited in CME00060, followed by the Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom.

holding.notes: The current Bodleian TEI record identifies manuscript_4492 / MS_Douce_114 and gives two institutional ARKs and Summary Catalogue number 21688.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Douce 114

physicalDescription.dimensions: Binding 9 x 6.625 inches; Vander Veen reports approximately 22 x 16 cm with a 15 x 10 cm writing space and 32 lines per page
physicalDescription.extent: ii later endleaves + 152 leaves in the current Bodleian record
physicalDescription.format: Codex; coloured capitals; nineteenth-century binding with marbled flyleaves reported by Vander Veen
notes: Horstmann and Vander Veen report 150 leaves, while the Bodleian record was revised in 2024 to ii + 152 leaves; the counting difference has not been reconciled.
Horstmann assigns Elizabeth to fols. 1-12, Christina to 12-26v, Marie to 26v-76, the Catherine letter to 76-89v, and Seven Points to 89v-148.
The Beauvale Charterhouse ownership inscription establishes provenance, not certain production there.

rights.basis: The texts and surviving witness were created in the fifteenth century; this assessment does not include modern photographs, editions, transcriptions, or provider terms.

next_steps: Re-run the exact Digital Bodleian shelfmark search and inspect the MMOL record for new digital-object links.
Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether an existing complete surrogate can be supplied before commissioning new photography.
If ordering, request the entire codex surface inventory and map the target fols. 1r-89v inside it rather than requesting only those leaves.
notes: No manuscript images are cached. The complete printed-source scans are not manuscript facsimiles.
openQuestions.question: Does a public or institutionally supplied complete physical-object facsimile of MS. Douce 114 exist, including covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, every leaf, and inserts?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian staff to confirm whether the four-text group ends on fol. 89v, as Horstmann reports, or fol. 87r, as Biblissima/JONAS reports.
Request a foliation crosswalk explaining the older 150-leaf count and current ii + 152-leaf catalog extent.
Use a future whole-codex facsimile to locate the closing compiler apology and shared opening of Seven Points precisely on fol. 89v.
notes: Until resolved, the lineage uses Horstmann's edition-reported fols. 1r-89v and labels the conflict rather than silently choosing a modern aggregation.
openQuestions.question: How should the target's final folio and the codex's total leaf count be reconciled across Horstmann, Biblissima/JONAS, Vander Veen, and the current Bodleian record?

next_steps: Consult Brown's full textual introduction and subsequent scholarship on the proposed Thurgarton and Beauvale contexts.
Compare the Middle English texts with the relevant Latin manuscript tradition and Brown's first-three critical edition.
Keep Beauvale's ownership inscription, the Rutland dialect localization, and any proposed production site as separate evidence until a transmission path is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Where, when, and from which Latin and earlier Middle English exemplars were the four translations produced and gathered before MS. Douce 114 was copied?

reviewNotes: The cataloged edited selection, its four medieval translated source texts, the manuscript codex, the 1885 article, digital encodings, scans, catalogs, and later scholarship are kept as distinct layers.
Complete cached sources preserve every provider-published canvas or page. The target article is associated through work_portion rather than cropped from either printed volume.
MS. Douce 114 is not represented by the cached edition facsimiles. A future manuscript delivery should cover the complete physical codex, not only fols. 1r-89v.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item CME00060, manually keyed from Carl Horstmann's 1885 Anglia article, pp. 102-196. The article presents German editorial matter and a complete edition of four related Middle English translations from Oxford, Bodleian Library MS. Douce 114: three saints' lives, Stefano Maconi's letter about Catherine of Siena, and the anonymous compiler-translator's framing apologies. Horstmann maps the edited manuscript selection to fols. 1r-89v of a substantially larger codex, with fol. 89v shared by the opening of the adjacent Seven Points of True Love. A complete 738-canvas scan of the entire printed Anglia volume and a second, volume-partial scan are cached with exact article mappings. No public whole-codex image object for Douce 114 was found on 2026-07-12; the exact negative search and a request route specifying every leaf and all codex furniture are recorded.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator/compiler; Latin source authors Philip of Clairvaux, Thomas of Cantimpré, Jacques de Vitry, and Stefano Maconi
catalogSummary.date: Middle English translations produced in the first quarter of the fifteenth century; surviving Douce 114 copy from the first half of the fifteenth century; edited selection published 1885
catalogSummary.region: England; language localized to the Rutland area and broadly Central Midlands, with Beauvale, Nottinghamshire established as later monastic provenance rather than certain production
catalogSummary.title: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114

catalogingSubject.label: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Anglia 8 (1885), pp. 102-196, and represented as CME00060: German editorial matter plus four related fifteenth-century Middle English translations and the anonymous compiler-translator's opening and closing apologies. The unit is not a single saint's life, not the entirety of MS. Douce 114, not the adjacent Seven Points of True Love, and not identical with Brown's later three-life critical edition.

contentParts.label: Horstmann's editorial frame and apparatus

locators.value: introduction pp. 102-106; headings and notes throughout pp. 102-196

notes: This is a conceptual, noncontiguous editorial part; its notes are interleaved with the source texts.
contentParts.summary: German title, manuscript and source introduction, supplied headings, footnotes, textual observations, and signed Berlin close.

contentParts.label: Þe Apologe of the compilour

locators.value: p. 107, shared with the opening of Elizabeth
at the opening of the fols. 1-12 Elizabeth item; exact ending not separately reported

notes: The XML nests this DIV3 inside the first numbered legend, but its translation policy frames the larger four-text group.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous compiler-translator explains sense-for-sense translation from Latin and selective omission of difficult scriptural authorities.

contentParts.label: Life of St. Elizabeth of Spalbeek

locators.value: fols. 1r-12; final side unspecified by Horstmann

contentParts.summary: Philip of Clairvaux's eyewitness-oriented account of Elizabeth's embodied Passion devotion, translated into Middle English and divided by the edition into six thematic chapters.

contentParts.label: Life of St. Christina Mirabilis

locators.value: fols. 12-26v; initial side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 134 / BSB canvas 148 is shared with the opening of Marie.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English translation of Thomas of Cantimpré's life of Christina the Astonishing, with a prologue and thirty-eight numbered chapters.

contentParts.label: Life of St. Marie d'Oignies

locators.value: 134-184; first explicit PB inside the division is 135 because p. 134 is shared
fols. 26v-76; final side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 184 / BSB canvas 198 is shared with the opening of the Catherine letter.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English translation of Jacques de Vitry's life of Marie d'Oignies, preceded by the English compiler's short prologue and arranged in two books of thirteen chapters each.

contentParts.label: Stefano Maconi's letter concerning St. Catherine of Siena

locators.value: 184-195; first explicit PB inside the division is 185 because p. 184 is shared
fols. 76-89v; initial side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 195 / BSB canvas 209 is shared with the closing compiler apology. Brown's later edition excludes this work.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English translation of Maconi's letter to Thomas Antonii of Siena recounting Catherine's deeds and virtues; it is epistolary testimony rather than a conventional full vita.

contentParts.label: A shorte Apologetik of þis englisshe compyloure

locators.value: 195-196; the only PB inside the division is 196 because p. 195 is shared
after the Catherine letter and before Seven Points on or near shared fol. 89v; exact lines unresolved

contentParts.summary: The compiler asks indulgence for stylistic variation and mixed northern and southern English, attributes faults to ignorance and obedient labor, and explains selected translation equivalents.

assertion.notes: This is a scholarly dating of the translations, not the dates of the Latin source works or the surviving copy.

dateStatements.display: First quarter of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Date of the Latin source letter. The surviving manuscript writes 1311; Horstmann corrects it to 1411 from the letter's historical context and Latin source.
Compilation and witness production are not assumed to be one event.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in the surviving Douce 114 grouping; the Catherine quire may have been added later

assertion.notes: The current repository record gives the broader fifteenth century; the narrower range is scholarly.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Douce 114

assertion.notes: This is the publication date of the cataloged edited selection, not a medieval work-composition date.

dateStatements.display: 1885, publication of Horstmann's four-text edited selection in Anglia 8

extent.description: Four numbered Middle English source items are framed by a German editorial introduction and a separately structured closing compiler apology; the opening apology is nested inside the first numbered division. Encoding metrics are one DIV1, six DIV2, eight DIV3, seventy DIV4 (six Elizabeth chapters, thirty-eight Christina chapters, and twenty-six Marie chapters), 106 P, 224 NOTE, and 95 PB elements covering printed pp. 102-196.

formStatements.description: The German editorial matter, Middle English lives, letter, and compiler apologies are prose; brief quotations do not make the unit mixed-form.

genres.label: Hagiographic letter
notes: The item is an epistolary testimony about Catherine's deeds and virtues rather than a full continuous vita.

scope.description: Horstmann's introduction, supplied headings, commentary, and notes

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The root XML LANG value names Middle English and German only, but direct inspection confirms substantive embedded Latin strings and quotations.

scope.description: Source titles, quotations, formulas, authorities, and editorial discussion embedded in German and Middle English prose

notes: The manuscript's f. 89v boundary is shared with an excluded adjacent work and must remain visible in any full-codex surrogate.
The XML's italic markup preserves Horstmann's abbreviation-expansion typography; it cannot answer paleographic questions about the original manuscript without images.
The record remains partial because the exact manuscript boundary, translation exemplars, and production context are unresolved and no public whole-codex facsimile was found.

next_steps: Consult Brown's full introduction and later scholarship on the proposed Thurgarton and Beauvale contexts.
Keep the Rutland linguistic localization, Central Midlands written standard, Beauvale provenance, and any institutional production claim separate.
Compare scribal, quire, and dialect evidence in a whole-codex facsimile before assigning a narrower composition or copying place.
notes: Horstmann's Nottinghamshire title is explicitly conjectural and based largely on later provenance.
openQuestions.question: Where were the four Middle English translations produced and gathered, as distinct from the later Beauvale ownership of MS. Douce 114?

next_steps: Request a Bodleian item-level foliation confirmation and a crosswalk for the closing apology and Seven Points opening.
Inspect a complete-codex facsimile without cropping the shared fol. 89v boundary.
Retain the edition-reported fols. 1r-89v mapping until stronger item-level evidence resolves the conflict.
openQuestions.question: Does the four-text group end on fol. 89v, as Horstmann reports, or fol. 87r, as Biblissima/JONAS reports, and where exactly does the closing compiler apology fall?

next_steps: Consult Brown's textual analysis of the first three lives and the relevant Latin manuscript census.
Research the Maconi letter's Latin transmission separately because Brown's edition excludes it.
Do not equate a close surviving Latin comparison manuscript with the translator's physical exemplar without transmission evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin manuscripts and lost or unidentified earlier Middle English copies stand behind each surviving Douce 114 translation?

assertion.notes: Vander Veen describes the manuscript as largely conforming to the fifteenth-century Central Midlands written standard, while the compiler himself acknowledges northern and southern mixture.
This is a linguistic localization reported from McIntosh, not proof of translation or copying at a specific institution.
The ownership inscription establishes that the codex belonged to Beauvale; it does not by itself prove production or translation there.

scope.description: Provenance of the manuscript witness, not the 1885 edition

notes: The translator calls himself simply learned, says he works sense-for-sense from Latin, and states that he undertook the labor at his prior's command.

notes: Brown attributes responsibility for gathering the four works to one compiler, while the surviving Catherine text may occupy a separately added quire.

notes: Horstmann is the modern editor, not the medieval author or translator.

scope.description: Latin source life of Elizabeth of Spalbeek

notes: This is source authorship, not authorship of the Middle English expression.
The Middle English compiler says he omits Jacques's long figurative Latin proem and substitutes a brief notice.
The letter is addressed to Thomas Antonii of Siena and is dated 26 October 1411; Horstmann corrects the manuscript's 1311 date.

scope.description: Latin letter concerning Catherine of Siena

subjects.label: Passion devotion and embodied imitation of Christ
Miracles, visions, prophecy, and extraordinary devotion

assertion.notes: Beauvale ownership and the compiler's obedience to a prior support Carthusian reception; exact translation and production location remain unresolved.

subjects.label: Catherine of Siena's deeds, virtues, and canonization testimony

summaries.value: Horstmann's edited selection presents three Middle English lives of holy women from the Liège region—Elizabeth of Spalbeek, Christina Mirabilis, and Marie d'Oignies—followed by Stefano Maconi's 1411 letter about Catherine of Siena. Anonymous compiler-translator apologies frame the group and explain sense-for-sense translation, selective omission of difficult scriptural authorities, obedience to a prior, mixed northern and southern English, and several translation choices. Horstmann supplies a German codicological and source introduction and extensive notes.

The encoded article contains Horstmann's introduction; an opening compiler apology; the Life of Elizabeth with six thematic chapters; a prologue and thirty-eight chapters of Christina; a compiler prologue and two thirteen-chapter books of Marie; Maconi's letter concerning Catherine; and the compiler's closing apology. The source-text boundaries share printed pp. 134, 184, and 195.

The manuscript witness continues beyond the cataloged selection with Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom, beginning on shared fol. 89v and extending to fol. 148r. That adjacent work is essential whole-codex context but is not part of CME00060. Brown's Three Women of Liège later re-edits only the first three lives and likewise is not coextensive with CME00060.

notes: The subtitle distinguishes this article from other Horstmann publications also headed Prosalegenden.
titles.value: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114

notes: The label is convenient but imprecise: the Catherine item is an epistolary testimony, not a full conventional vita.
titles.value: The Lives of Four Female Saints

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator/compiler; Latin source authors Philip of Clairvaux, Thomas of Cantimpré, Jacques de Vitry, and Stefano Maconi
work.dateDisplay: Middle English translations produced in the first quarter of the fifteenth century; surviving Douce 114 copy from the first half of the fifteenth century; edited selection published 1885

work.preferredTitle: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
work.regionDisplay: England; language localized to the Rutland area and broadly Central Midlands, with Beauvale, Nottinghamshire established as later monastic provenance rather than certain production
